text: The surgeon mobilizes the colon to identify the ureter, which is visibly peristalsing. The ureter is circumferentially mobilized with care to minimize trauma. The ovarian pedicles are divided, improving the exposure of the ureter. The ureter is then dissected down towards the bladder.
The surgeon mobilizes the ovary, exposing the round ligament. The round ligament is cauterized with a bipolar device before being divided, aiding in the mobilization of the ureter.
The surgeon enters the retropubic space, revealing the pubic symphysis. The bladder is partially mobilized, with one side dropped while remaining attached at the midline.
The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery and divides it between clips, aiding in the exposure of the ureter. Node dissection is performed, focusing on the external iliac nodes.

The surgeon identifies the genital femoral nerve, marking the lateral boundary for the node dissection. Clips are used to control the boundaries of the node dissection, focusing on nodes lateral to the external iliac artery.

The surgeon pushes the ureter aside to remove nodal packets from the external iliac artery and vein. The obturator nerve is exposed for the first time, with the distal end of the node dissection visible.
The surgeon places larger nodal packets into a retrieval bag for later removal. The pedicle to the bladder is visualized and divided between clips, with a stapler used if the pedicle is large.

The surgeon mobilizes the right ureter and divides the round ligament on the right side. The peritoneum is cut lateral to the medial umbilical ligament to enter the spate serratius. The external iliac artery is mobilized for node dissection, with nodes lateral to the artery being divided and vessels separated.

The surgeon isolates the pedicle to the bladder lateral to the ureter, using hemlock clips for division. Lymphatic packages are removed, and the obturator nerve is clearly visible. The uterus and ovaries are elevated, revealing the pouch of Douglas. The peritoneum is divided, exposing underlying structures.

The assistant places a sponge stick into the vagina, which is visible as the surgeon cuts down on it to enter the vaginal canal. The hypervascular vaginal wall is cauterized using bipolar electrocautery before division. The left ureter is divided between hemlock clips.

The surgeon uses hemlock clips to divide the right ureter, ensuring no urine leaks into the surgical field. A tissue margin is sent for frozen section analysis. The vaginal wall is cauterized with bipolar electrocautery and then divided using monopolar electrocautery.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis, maintaining a dry surgical field. The left vaginal wall has been cut, and the right vaginal wall is now being cut similarly, with the sponge stick visible in the vagina. The median umbilical ligament is divided, allowing the bladder to drop completely, revealing the dorsal vein complex.

The surgeon controls the dorsal vein complex using bipolar cautery and cuts it with monopolar cautery, exposing the urethra. A Foley catheter is visible, and the surgeon carefully divides it while maintaining the balloon's inflation by squeezing the catheter internally. Gentle traction on the catheter reveals the urethra, which is brought into the surgical field for complete removal with the cystectomy specimen.

The surgeon visualizes the urethra to determine the precise cutting location. Once the specimen is fully detached, it is pulled out through the vagina using an instrument placed by the assistant. The vaginal wall is mobilized for a clamshell-style repair using V-lock sutures, starting in the middle and running laterally to create a watertight anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the right side using a knot to secure the V-lock suture. A tunnel is dissected underneath the sigmoid mesentery, crossing the midline with robotic instruments. The left ureter is passed through this tunnel to the right side, aligning both ureters. A measuring tape is used to gauge the bowel length, cut to fifteen centimeters. Two silk sutures are placed on the proximal and distal ends of the bowel conduit, and a 45mm laparoscopic stapler is used to divide the bowel.

The surgeon applies a second bowel stapler to further isolate the segment intended for the ileal conduit. The mesentery is cut to mobilize the bowel, ensuring approximately five centimeters of bowel can be wasted for stoma planning. The mesentery is mobilized without cutting into it on the stoma side, preparing for a side-to-side functional end-to-end anastomosis.

A small opening is made into the bowel, with the tail of the bowel left intact to serve as a handle during manipulation. A 45mm laparoscopic stapler is applied to the anti-mesenteric border of the bowel, fired twice to create a broad anastomotic surface. The hole in the anastomosis is closed using a 60mm stapler, completing the bowel anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the mesenteric trap to prevent an internal hernia. On the ureteral side of the conduit, a staple line is visible, which is excluded using a running two-ovicral suture to minimize the risk of future stone formation. The crotch of the bowel anastomosis is reinforced with visible stitches, and the conduit is checked to ensure the mesentery is not twisted.

The surgeon prepares the stoma side by cutting away five centimeters, maturing the stoma. The ureteral side is identified, and a hole is created for the ureteral enteroconastomosis. The right ureter is cut and spatulated, with the distal ureter left in place as a handle. A six o'clock stitch is placed at the crotch of the spatulation, and the handle is cut away and sent to pathology as the final ureteral margin. A twelve o'clock stitch is then placed.

The surgeon closes one side of the anastomosis by running a suture from the twelve o'clock to the six o'clock position, tying the first stitch to the second. The anastomosis is flipped, revealing the ureter. A wire is passed up the right ureter, and a seven-centimeter single J stent is inserted. The tip of the stent is cut to fit, as the original ninety-centimeter stent is too long.
The surgeon secures the stent to the conduit using 4-0 chromic sutures. Floral monocryl sutures are run along the bowel and ureter to complete the ureteral enteric anastomosis.

The surgeon performs the left ureteral anastomosis by cutting and spatulating the ureter, then creating a small opening in the ileal conduit. The first foramenocral suture is placed at the crotch of the spatulation, avoiding back walling. The handle is cut away and sent as the final ureteral margin. Six and twelve o'clock stitches are placed, with the twelve o'clock stitch run down to the six o'clock stitch and tied, completing half the anastomosis. A wire is passed up the left ureter, and a seven French single J stent is inserted over the wire, cut to fit, and secured with fluorochromic sutures. The stoma end of the conduit is tagged to facilitate its exteriorization. The pelvis is dry with no bleeding, marking the completion of the robotic portion of the operation.
